content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1109 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

March 29, 1976

A96-606

Assemblyman Lawrence Kapiloff
State Capitol
Sacramento CA 95814

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 76-034

Dear Assemblyman Kapiloff:

Thank you for your letter of March 11, 1976, posing a
number of questions related to activities contemplated by
one of your opponents in the upcoming election. The Political
Reform Act provides that "any person may request the Commission to issue an opinion with respect to his duties under
[the Act]." Government Code Section 83114. The Commission
has interpreted this section to mean opinion requests may be
submitted by "any person whose duties under the Act are in
question or by that person's representative." 2 Cal.Adm.
Code Section 18320(a). Since your questions involve the
activities of your opponent and his potential obligations
under the Political Reform Act, no formal opinion will be
issued. I hope, however, that the following informal comments will be helpful.

In questions No. 2, 4 and 6, you ask whether various
activities related to advertising commercial products and
sponsoring an event designed to celebrate the Bicentennial
can constitute in-kind contributions to a candidate who
participates in the commercials and the celebration. The
Political Reform Act defines a "contribution" to be a "payment
· · · " except to the extent that full and adequate consideration
is received unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is made for political purposes." Government
Code Section 82015. In the activities you describe, your
opponent undoubtedly is providing full and adequate consideration
in return for the payments he receives from the commercial
sponsor. Furthermore, it would not appear that the purpose
of the commercials or the Bicentennial celebration are political
within the meaning of the Act. To the contrary, the commercials
are designed to sell a product and the celebration seems to
be for the purpose of commemorating the Bicentennial. Accordingly,
the funds expended on these events would not be contributions
to your opponent.

The image appears to be a blank or nearly blank page with only a few faint circular marks visible on the right side. There is no substantial text content to transcribe from this document.

Assemblyman Kapiloff
March 29, 1976
Page Two


In questions No. 1, 3 and 5, you ask about the equal
time doctrine and the proper tax treatment of political
contributions. Neither of these subjects are addressed by
the Political Reform Act and it, therefore, would be in-
appropriate for me to comment upon them.

If you wish to appeal the denial of your request for an
opinion, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal.Adm. Code Section
18321(a). If you have any further questions, please contact
Kenneth Finney, Chief of the Commission's Legal Division.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Daniel H. Lowenstein,
Chairman

DHL:pvp

I can see this appears to be a mostly blank page with only a few small curved marks or symbols visible on the right side at different vertical positions. The page appears to be white/light gray with minimal content.

Since there is essentially no legible text content to transcribe on this document, there is nothing to return per your instructions.

The Political Reform Act provides that "any person may request the Commission to issue an opinion with respect to his duties under [the Act]." Government Code Section 83114. The Commission has interpreted this section to mean opinion requests may be submitted by "any person whose duties under the Act are in question or by that person's representative." 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18320(a). Since your questions involve the activities of your opponent and his potential obligations under the Political Reform Act, no formal opinion will be issued. I hope, however, that the following informal comments will be helpful. In questions No. 2, 4 and 6, you ask whether various activities related to advertising commercial products and sponsoring an event designed to celebrate the Bicentennial can constitute in-kind contributions to a candidate who participates in the commercials and the celebration. The Political Reform Act defines a "contribution" to be a "payment ... except to the extent that full and adequate consideration is received unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes." Government Code Section 82015. In the activities you describe, your opponent undoubtedly is providing full and adequate consideration in return for the payments he receives from the commercial sponsor. To the contrary, the commercials are designed to sell a product and the celebration seems to be for the purpose of commemorating the Bicentennial. Accordingly, the funds expended on these events would not be contributions to your opponent.
